sections.header: Verses 1-4 — "I Knew That You Are a Gracious God"

heb.paragraph: Jonah quotes the great self-revelation of God from Exodus 34:6-7, the most cited creed in the Hebrew Bible. But where Moses received this as good news, Jonah hurls it as an accusation. The same divine attributes that form the foundation of Israel's hope become grounds for the prophet's rage when extended to Nineveh.
וַיֵּרַע אֶל־יוֹנָה (wayyēraʿ ʾel-yōnāh) = it was evil/displeasing to Jonah

refs.note: The original self-revelation of YHWH to Moses: 'The LORD, the LORD, the compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in love.' Jonah quotes this word for word but as accusation rather than praise.
Moses appeals to Exodus 34:6 to intercede for rebellious Israel. Jonah knows the formula works — which is exactly why he fled.
Elijah, like Jonah, asked God to take his life in despair. Both prophets reached a point where death seemed preferable to continuing.

notes.note: Jonah was greatly displeased and angry. His anger was not righteous indignation but sinful resentment. He wanted Nineveh destroyed and was furious that God showed mercy to Israel's enemies.

This verse reveals Jonah's motive for fleeing: he knew God's character and feared exactly what happened. His theology was correct but his application was nationalistic. He wanted divine mercy restricted to Israel.

Jonah's death wish echoes Elijah's in 1 Kings 19:4, but where Elijah despaired because of persecution, Jonah despaired because of mercy. He would rather die than live in a world where God is gracious to pagans.
God's question 'Is it right for you to be angry?' is gentle pastoral confrontation. It does not condemn but invites self-examination.

Calvin identifies Jonah's error as restricting God's universal sovereignty to national boundaries. The prophet confused election with exclusion, assuming that God's covenant with Israel meant rejection of all others. Calvin reads this as a warning against spiritual possessiveness.

Calvin notes that Jonah's death wish is the extreme consequence of theological resentment. When a person's identity is built on the exclusion of others from grace, the extension of that grace becomes an existential threat.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Jonah's quotation of Exod 34:6 is nearly verbatim, adding only 'who relents from sending calamity' (niham al haraʿah). This addition is precisely the attribute Jonah resents: God's willingness to relent from judgment. The creed that is Israel's greatest comfort becomes this prophet's greatest grievance.

The question hayyēṭēb ḥarah lak ('is it right for you to be angry?') uses the same root (yrh, Hiphil) as the narrator's description of Jonah's displeasure in v. 1. God echoes Jonah's own emotional language back to him.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart identifies this as the theological key to the entire book. Jonah's quotation of Exodus 34:6-7 — the 'grace formula' that appears in whole or part at least eleven times in the OT — reveals that his flight was premeditated theological protest. He knew God would be merciful, and he opposed that mercy on nationalistic grounds. Stuart reads this as a critique of exclusivist theology within Israel: the book asks whether YHWH's covenant love (hesed) has boundaries.

Stuart compares Jonah's death wish to Elijah's (1 Kgs 19:4) and Moses' (Num 11:15), noting that all three prophets reached moments of existential crisis. But while Elijah and Moses were exhausted by opposition, Jonah is enraged by success. God's response — a question, not a rebuke — is pedagogical: the divine teacher begins to lead the student toward a new understanding.

hist.context: This chapter is the theological climax of the book. Jonah's angry prayer reveals that his original flight was not cowardice but theological protest: he knew God was merciful and feared that preaching to Nineveh would result in their deliverance. He quotes Exodus 34:6 — the foundational creed of Israelite theology — not as praise but as complaint. The irony is profound: the attributes that sustained Israel through exile, rebellion, and judgment are the very attributes Jonah resents when applied to outsiders. God's question 'Is it right for you to be angry?' remains unanswered, hanging in the air until the book's final verse.

sections.header: Verses 5-11 — The Vine, the Worm, and the Question

heb.paragraph: The exact identification is uncertain — proposals include the castor-oil plant (Ricinus communis) and the bottle gourd. The plant is one of four agents God 'appoints' (manah) in the book: the fish (1:17), the plant (4:6), the worm (4:7), and the east wind (4:8). Together they form a quartet of sovereign provision.

heb.gloss: to pity / to have compassion on
heb.paragraph: The verb appears only in vv. 10-11 and nowhere else in the book. God's closing argument hinges on this word: if Jonah 'pitied' (has) a plant he did not create or cultivate, should not God 'pity' a city of 120,000 people? The qal wahomer (lesser to greater) argument is devastating in its simplicity.

refs.note: The elder brother's anger at the father's mercy toward the prodigal son parallels Jonah's anger at God's mercy toward Nineveh. Both narratives end with a question from the father/God.
'Who are you, a human being, to talk back to God?' Paul's argument about divine sovereignty over mercy echoes God's closing question to Jonah.
'My thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways' — the principle that Jonah's story dramatizes: God's mercy exceeds human categories.

notes.note: Jonah sat down east of the city to watch what would happen, still hoping God might destroy Nineveh. His obedience in preaching was mechanical, not heartfelt.

God used four appointed agents in this book — a fish, a vine, a worm, and a wind — each sovereignly provided to teach his prophet. The vine brought comfort; its removal brought suffering. Both served God's pedagogical purpose.

God's argument is from lesser to greater (qal wahomer): if Jonah cared about a temporary plant, how much more should God care about 120,000 human beings created in his image? The reference to people 'who cannot tell their right hand from their left' likely refers to young children, emphasizing Nineveh's innocents.
Calvin sees in the vine a parable of all earthly comforts: they are given by God, enjoyed briefly, and removed at God's discretion. The one who rages at their loss reveals misplaced affections.

Calvin regards God's final question as the theological summit of the book. The Creator has rights over his creation that the creature cannot comprehend. If human beings feel compassion for things they did not make, how much greater is the compassion of the one who made all things? The book ends with a question because the answer must come from the reader's own conscience.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb manah ('appoint/provide') appears here for the second time (cf. 1:17), governing the vine as it governed the fish. God appoints a worm (v. 7) and a wind (v. 8) with the same verb. The four uses of manah create a theological framework: every element of the natural world serves God's sovereign purpose.

The phrase 'who cannot tell their right hand from their left' is debated: it may refer to young children (under the age of moral accountability), to moral ignorance more broadly, or to the entire population's lack of spiritual discernment. If children, the number 120,000 would suggest a total population of several hundred thousand.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart reads the vine episode as an enacted parable. The four appointed agents (fish, vine, worm, wind) demonstrate comprehensive divine sovereignty: God controls marine life, vegetation, insects, and weather. Each agent serves the larger argument about God's right to show compassion. Stuart notes that the scorching east wind (ruah qadhim) is the same sirocco that afflicts Israel in prophetic judgment passages (Hos 13:15; Jer 4:11), adding another layer: Jonah experiences on his own body the kind of judgment he wanted inflicted on Nineveh.

Stuart identifies God's closing question as a qal wahomer argument (from lesser to greater), one of the standard forms of rabbinic reasoning. The argument is unanswerable: if a human can pity a plant, the Creator must be permitted to pity a city. The open ending forces the reader — especially the Israelite reader — to confront the scope of divine mercy. Stuart argues that this is the book's ultimate purpose: not to tell the story of Jonah but to ask Israel whether YHWH's compassion has limits.

hist.context: The book's final scene is a masterclass in divine pedagogy. God provides an object lesson: a fast-growing vine gives Jonah shade and comfort; a worm destroys it; a scorching east wind compounds his misery. When Jonah is angry enough to die over the vine, God delivers the closing argument: 'You have been concerned about this vine, though you did not tend it or make it grow. But Nineveh has more than 120,000 people who cannot tell their right hand from their left — and also many animals. Should I not have concern for that great city?' The book ends with a question, not an answer. The reader must supply the response. This open ending is one of the most theologically sophisticated literary devices in the Hebrew Bible.

rows.label: vv.1-4
rows.text: The Prophet's Rage: Exodus 34:6 as Accusation

rows.label: vv.5-11
rows.text: The Object Lesson: Vine, Worm, Wind, and Question

lit.note: Chapter 4 mirrors chapter 2 structurally: both contain a prayer from Jonah and a divine response. But where chapter 2 was thanksgiving, chapter 4 is accusation. The book moves from Jonah fleeing God's mercy (ch. 1) to receiving it (ch. 2) to witnessing it (ch. 3) to resenting it (ch. 4).

themes.note: Mercy peaks at 10: this chapter is the book's meditation on the scope of divine compassion. Sovereignty at 9: God appoints vine, worm, and wind to teach his prophet. Covenant at 7: the Exodus 34:6 quotation places covenant theology at the center. Justice at 7: the tension between justice (Nineveh deserves destruction) and mercy (God relents) is the driving conflict.

hebtext.note: Jonah quotes the great self-revelation of God from Exodus 34:6-7, the most cited creed in the Hebrew Bible. But where Moses received this as good news, Jonah hurls it as an accusation. The same divine attributes that form the foundation of Israel's hope become grounds for the prophet's rage when extended to Nineveh.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Jonah's Anger and God's Compassion

translations.text: Then the Lord God provided a leafy plant and made it grow up over Jonah to give shade for his head to ease his discomfort, and Jonah was very happy about the plant.
Now the LORD God appointed a plant and made it come up over Jonah, that it might be a shade over his head, to save him from his discomfort. So Jonah was exceedingly glad because of the plant.
Now, Lord, take away my life, for it is better for me to die than to live.”
Therefore now, O LORD, please take my life from me, for it is better for me to die than to live.”

Reads Jonah's Anger and God's Compassion within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: The original self-revelation of YHWH to Moses: 'The LORD, the LORD, the compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in love.' Jonah quotes this word for word but as accusation rather than praise.
Moses appeals to Exodus 34:6 to intercede for rebellious Israel. Jonah knows the formula works — which is exactly why he fled.
Elijah, like Jonah, asked God to take his life in despair. Both prophets reached a point where death seemed preferable to continuing.
The elder brother's anger at the father's mercy toward the prodigal son parallels Jonah's anger at God's mercy toward Nineveh. Both narratives end with a question from the father/God.
'Who are you, a human being, to talk back to God?' Paul's argument about divine sovereignty over mercy echoes God's closing question to Jonah.
'My thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways' — the principle that Jonah's story dramatizes: God's mercy exceeds human categories.

coaching.tip: God's final question — 'Should I not have concern for the great city of Nineveh, in which there are more than 120,000 people who cannot tell their right hand from their left?' — is left unanswered. The book ends with a question mark. Jonah never responds. The reader must answer instead: does God's compassion have ethnic limits? The silence demands a verdict.
